profile.name: Kristijan Ilie
profile.tagline: KQL enthusiast building AI-augmented workflows, IP enrichment tooling, and a playbook for every alert imaginable.

bec.attackNarrative:
Between 7 and 10 March 2026, an unauthorised party gained access to the email account of Donald
Anderson and used it to attempt a business email compromise (BEC) — a fraud in which an attacker,
operating from a legitimate internal account, conducts deceptive correspondence with a third party.
Over this period the account was accessed from multiple external IP addresses inconsistent with the
user's normal activity.

Early in the intrusion, the attacker created a hidden mailbox rule designed to conceal incoming replies
from the targeted correspondent (Naomi Hunter), ensuring the legitimate account owner would not see
the fraudulent exchange. The attacker then accessed and downloaded a number of files from
SharePoint, created and sent an email titled "Report" from the compromised account, and subsequently
deleted the sent message to avoid detection — behaviour consistent with a deliberate, staged
operation rather than opportunistic access.

The activity went undetected for approximately five days until 15 March 2026, when a suspicious email
reported to the administrator prompted investigation. The compromised account was contained and its
password reset. The mechanism of initial access and whether any downloaded data left the
environment remain under review.

timeline.activity: First unauthorised sign-in using the Donald account
timeline.detail: IP: 54[.]206[.]115[.]107 Client: donald[.]anderson[@]wdlabs[.]com[.]au Extra contexts: Signed in via OWA, opening the Donald account mailbox for initial access.

IP: 91[.]217[.]249[.]193 Client: donald[.]anderson[@]wdlabs[.]com[.]au Extra contexts: Donald account observed accessing multiple different emails to observe for reconnaissance to gather information about the victim asset.

timeline.activity: Among emails being accessed Donald account created new Inbox Rule
timeline.detail: IP: 91[.]217[.]249[.]193:17130 Client: donald[.]anderson[@]wdlabs[.]com[.]au Extra contexts: Email rule was created called “.” to move emails from Naomi account to RSS feeds and Mark as Read. This means if Donald glanced at the folder list, nothing would draw his eye. Additionally, the unauthorised party made sure to prevent further rules from applying to surface the mail when it has been intentionally hidden by this first rule. T1564.008 —(Email Hiding Rules)

timeline.activity: Donald's account accessed the company sharepoint
timeline.detail: IP: 45[.]133[.]5[.]24 Client: donald[.]anderson[@]wdlabs[.]com[.]au Extra contexts: The Donald account searched and downloaded various company SharePoint files.

timeline.activity: Donald’s account authored an unauthorised email
timeline.detail: IP: 104[.]234[.]32[.]142 Client: donald[.]anderson[@]wdlabs[.]com[.]au Extra contexts: The Donald account created a email with Exchange Item ID “LgAAAAB7v8slKtVETrcRaS1f5gjaAQADbzp KDkyFTZHbmG6YAd29AAAAAAEPAAAB” in the Drafts folder of the Donald account mailbox.

timeline.activity: Donald’s account accessed SharePoint and viewed recently used/created documents
timeline.detail: IP: 173[.]239[.]229[.]83 Client: donald[.]anderson[@]wdlabs[.]com[.]au Extra contexts: The Donald account performed reconnaissance against SharePoint, using Donald’s recent-document history to identify high-value content and enumerating SitePages by author to map internal roles and relationships which is consistent with target selection for a Business Email Compromise. T1213 — Data from Information Repositories T1087 — Account Discovery
An email titled ‘Report’ was sent from the account (Send), from 104[.]234[.]32[.]143. The fraudulent message to the counterparty Naomi Hunter appears to be the objective of the BEC.
An item was moved to Deleted Items (MoveToDeletedItems), from 104[.]234[.]32[.]143. Send-then-delete is concealment, consistent with the earlier inboxrule behaviour.
Admin Dalan began investigating a report of a suspicious email from Donald Anderson to Naomi Hunter. ~5 days between last attacker activity (10 Mar) and detection.
Account password reset. Remediation actions completed (no log timestamp — response action, not an observed attack event).

bec.initialAccess:
On 7 March 2026 12:49:55 PM GMT10+, the first unauthorised sign-in to the Donald Account from IP
address 54[.]206[.]115[.]107 (AWS IP) via Outlook Web App (OWA).
Following authentication, the session accessed five email items.
The sign-in originated from infrastructure inconsistent with the account’s established baseline.
bec.unauthorisedActivity:
On 9 March 2026 at 5:10:45 PM GMT10+ the Donald Account created a new inbox rule named “.”.
From the IP address 91[.]217[.]249[.]193 (Germany, Berlin – VPN provider) The inbox rule moved all
incoming emails from Naomi.Hunter@wdlabs.com.au marked them as read and moved them to the
‘RSS Feeds’ folder. [Comment: The inbox rule was created before any unauthorised outbound email
was observed, indicating it was established preparatory to the fraudulent activity rather than in
reaction to it. By automatically removing Naomi Hunter's incoming messages from view, the rule
ensured that once the unauthorised party began corresponding with Naomi using the Donald account,
the legitimate account holder would not see her replies — allowing the fraudulent exchange to
continue undetected. ]
bec.dataAccess:
Two days after initial access, on 9 March 2026, the unauthorised party authenticated as the Donald
account conducted the most significant data-access activity of the incident. Between 5:20 PM and
5:26 PM, the session performed a series of SharePoint/OneDrive operations recorded as
FilePreviewed (16 events), FileAccessed (17 events) and, most significantly, FileDownloaded (13
events) — twelve files downloaded from IP 45[.]133[.]5[.]24 and 45[.]133[.]5[.]15 on 9 March, and one
further download from 173[.]239[.]229[.]83 on 10 March.
On the mail side, MailItemsAccessed events were recorded across 7–10 March (8 events), consistent
with the session reading mailbox contents. On 10 March the session also created an item (7:33:45
PM), sent an item (Send, 7:51:40 PM) and subsequently moved an item to Deleted Items
(MoveToDeletedItems, 8:15:56 PM).
[Comment: The volume and clustering of FileDownloaded events represents the clearest evidence of
potential exfiltration in the incident — these are full file retrievals, distinct from the
FilePreviewed/FileAccessed events which indicate viewing only. The Send followed by
MoveToDeletedItems on 10 March is consistent with the unauthorised party sending mail from the
account and then deleting it from the Sent/visible folders to conceal the activity, consistent with the
earlier inbox-rule concealment behaviour.]
bec.lessonsLearned:
I think I learned a lot about how to do a proper investigation and putting it into a report that is
detailed. I accept my documentation is good but the way I do it (template it) needs to be
better. To me the important thing is to learn the process, improve the process and make
sure its replicable. And then (ideally) to automate the process.

I learned a great deal about the different views in Sentinel and where to look for indicators of
compromise. I used my natural curiosity to correlate different pieces of information like time,
date, client ip, location, with public resources like abuseipdb or ip-api to a known indicator of
compromise and used that to inform the rest of my investigation.

I learned a lot about how to use KQL and how to correlate different bits of useful information
in Sentinel’s KQL to search for additional instances of malicious activity that would not have
been possible if I didn’t double check for additional activity performed by client session IDs.
I think what I would do differently is create & use a findings/reporting template so that I can
inform future investigations more cleanly and more effectively.

Obsidian is fine for note-taking, but I need to perhaps focus on translating the Excel
spreadsheets I curate into something that explains itself better, not just looks pretty.
And to provide that additional context throughout instead of a little bit here and little bit there.
And I learned about defanging IPs to prevent accidental clicks on malicious URLs during
threat analysis.

Lastly, A middle-ground KQL trick if I want readable output without losing fields: use `projectreorder` or `project-away` instead. `project-reorder TimeGenerated, Operation, UserId,
ClientIP` puts the columns I care about first but keeps everything else still hanging off the
row. `project-away` lets me drop just the noise (e.g. `OrganizationId`, `RecordType`) while
keeping the rest. Best of both worlds when I’m still in discovery mode.
